claims_verified.claim: A new GitOps.sync_worktree_to_origin fetches origin and hard-resets the persisted task worktree to origin/<branch> before spec_sha is computed, closing the stale-worktree gap
claims_verified.evidence: orchestrator/gitops.py:123-152 (fetch + rev-parse remote ref + hard reset, False when branch absent on origin)

claims_verified.claim: _phase_resume calls the sync AFTER all validation (empty reason, terminal exists, terminal resumable) and BEFORE computing spec_sha / appending the director_resume event
claims_verified.evidence: orchestrator/run.py:433-442 - sync_worktree_to_origin(wt, task_id) precedes blob_sha() and append_log()

claims_verified.claim: Sync does not run when validation fails (e.g. still-running task or PATH_GUARD_VIOLATION), so a live/poisoned worktree is never hard-reset
claims_verified.evidence: tests/test_run_cli.py:1012-1028 test_resume_does_not_sync_worktree_when_validation_fails monkeypatches sync_worktree_to_origin and asserts it's never called when the terminal is non-resumable

claims_verified.claim: My exact reported scenario (VPS worktree stale at CAP_REACHED, Director pushes a spec correction from a separate clone, then --resume) now delivers the corrected spec, records the correct spec_sha, and the local worktree ends up built on top of the Director's commit (no non-fast-forward push rejection)
claims_verified.evidence: tests/test_run_cli.py:1059-1106 test_resume_fetches_director_spec_correction_pushed_from_a_separate_clone: asserts worktree spec content flips from stale to corrected, spec_sha matches the corrected blob's real git hash-object, and `git merge-base HEAD director_tip == director_tip` (HEAD descends from the Director's pushed commit)

claims_verified.claim: Unit-level sync behavior (fast-forward when remote branch has moved; no-op when branch absent on origin) is covered directly on GitOps
Full suite green after the fix
claims_verified.evidence: `python3 -m pytest -q` -> 733 passed; targeted resume/sync tests -> 13 passed

test_assessment: The prior blocker (stale persisted worktree causing the resumed spec correction to be silently dropped and the eventual push to be rejected non-fast-forward) is directly fixed and directly tested: an integration test reproduces the exact multi-clone scenario I verified live in the previous round, plus focused unit tests on the new sync_worktree_to_origin covering both the fast-forward case and the branch-absent no-op case, plus a negative test proving the sync never fires on a refused (still-running/poisoned) resume.
residual_risks: sync_worktree_to_origin runs outside the run_lock held later by _phase_loop; two concurrent `--phase resume` invocations on the same task could race on the hard reset. This is a narrow, self-inflicted scenario (the Director would have to issue two overlapping resume commands) and is outside the scope of the finding I raised, so not blocking here.
